title: On what cannot be delegated even when it could be
summary: Argue that there is a category of acts — caring for a dying parent, apologising in person, making a difficult decision — that retain their moral character only when performed by the person to whom they belong, even in a world where delegation is technically possible.
topic: The moral significance of acts that cannot be delegated without changing their meaning
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay arguing that some acts — apologising in person, being present at a dying bedside, making a decision that affects others — cannot be delegated without losing their essential moral character, even when practical delegation would be entirely possible. Argue that the moral value of such acts lies not just in their outcome but in their being performed by the person to whom they morally belong. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid making this an essay about care work or labour. Treat the question philosophically: what does personal performance add that delegation removes? 5–7 paragraphs.
hintStarters: Not everything that can be outsourced should be, and the reason for this is not always efficiency or quality but…
What delegation removes from an act is not always visible in the outcome but in the…
Granted, the practical case for delegation is often overwhelming — time, skill, access, resources all conspire to make it rational. Even so,…

quyền sở hữu đạo đức
trung lập về kết quả

làm chứng, có mặt chứng kiến

hintVocab.phrase: to accept the cost of an act
hintVocab.meaningVi: chấp nhận chi phí của một hành động
nghĩa vụ phải có mặt
sự trung thành trong mối quan hệ
logic của sự thay thế
đại diện, uỷ quyền

hintVocab.phrase: to be present in one's own person
hintVocab.meaningVi: có mặt bằng chính con người của mình

hintVocab.phrase: the cost of doing it oneself
hintVocab.meaningVi: chi phí của việc tự làm
sự nghiêm túc đạo đức được thể hiện cụ thể

sampleText:
There is a category of acts that technical efficiency cannot rescue. One can hire a skilled carer; one cannot hire someone to grieve on one's behalf. One can send a representative to a difficult meeting; one cannot send them to apologise in one's name and have the apology mean what an apology means. Not everything that can be outsourced should be, and the reason is not always about quality of outcome — it is about who the outcome belongs to, and what performing the act says about the person to whom it morally belongs.

The modern default favours delegation wherever delegation works. This is sensible as a practical heuristic: one delegates what others can do at least as well, in order to attend to what only one can do. The logic is sound when applied to genuinely interchangeable acts — tasks whose value is entirely in the output and for which the agent is irrelevant. But not all acts are of this kind. Some are what might be called irreducibly first-personal: their significance lies precisely in the fact that they are performed by the person for whom they are appropriate, and not by a substitute.

Not everything that can be outsourced should be, and the reason for this is not always efficiency or quality but meaning. An apology delivered by a representative communicates something, but it does not communicate an apology in the full sense. An apology is not merely information — 'I acknowledge that I caused harm' — but a form of self-submission in which the person who caused the harm accepts the discomfort of standing before the person they harmed and offering themselves to be seen as the cause. The representative who delivers the message does not submit; the apologiser remains at a comfortable distance and sends their proxy into the discomfort they owe. What arrives is not an apology but a message about an apology.

Granted, the practical case for delegation is often overwhelming — time, skill, access, resources all conspire to make it rational. Even so, the practical case does not exhaust the moral case. There are moments when the moral significance of an act lies precisely in its cost to the person performing it — in the fact that they had to rearrange themselves, that they had to be present in their own person, that they accepted the difficulty of doing it themselves rather than having it done. When a person travels six hours to be at the bedside of a dying parent, the journey is part of the act. Not because it produces a better outcome — a phone call might have been equally comforting — but because it makes visible, concretely and physically, the weight the relationship had.

What delegation removes from an act is not always visible in the outcome but in the expressive dimension — the part of the act that communicates what the actor takes the situation to require of them. This is what is lost when decisions affecting other people are delegated: the people affected do not receive only the decision; they also receive a statement about how seriously the decision-maker took the obligation to make the decision themselves. The manager who delegates a redundancy conversation to human resources is not merely being efficient; they are making a statement about the relationship between themselves and the person they are letting go. Whether they intend to or not, they are saying: you are not owed my presence in this.

It is the expressive dimension of certain acts, not their practical content, that explains why delegation feels like an evasion when it should not be. The outcomes may be identical; the meaning is not. A letter of condolence written by an assistant and signed by an employer contains the same propositions as one written by the employer themselves; what it does not contain is the evidence that the employer found the writing necessary — that they sat with the difficulty of what to say. This is not sentimentalism; it is an accurate account of what some acts are communicating beyond their propositional content.

None of this argues against delegation in general. Most of what we delegate, we are right to delegate, and the world works because of it. The question is whether we have correctly identified which acts belong to the category that delegation cannot enter without changing what the act means. The answer requires not efficiency calculations but a question about moral ownership: whose act is this, and what does it mean for that person to have it done for them rather than by them?

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Negative sentence inversion with 'Not everything that can be outsourced should be'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Not everything that can be outsourced should be' sử dụng đảo ngữ ẩn: cấu trúc 'Not everything X should Y' đặt sự phủ định ở đầu, tạo ra một lập luận bằng cách từ chối kết luận hiển nhiên. Đây không phải là đảo ngữ phụ trợ cổ điển nhưng là đảo ngữ của sự nhấn mạnh — đặt 'Not' trước chủ ngữ tạo ra nhịp điệu và quyền lực biện luận mạnh mẽ ở C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Not everything that can be outsourced should be' performs fronted negation — placing the negation first, before the subject, rather than the default 'Everything that can be outsourced should not necessarily be.' This creates a strong argumentative rhythm and immediately establishes the essay's contrarian stance, characteristic of C1 argumentative prose.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate the expressive dimension as the real explanatory factor
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the expressive dimension of certain acts, not their practical content, that explains why delegation feels like an evasion when it should not be' dùng cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để bác bỏ giải thích thực tế (nội dung thực tế) và thay thế bằng giải thích biểu đạt. Đây là chiến lược giữa đoạn 5 và 6: chuyển từ minh hoạ sang chẩn đoán.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the expressive dimension of certain acts, not their practical content, that explains why delegation feels like an evasion when it should not be' uses the cleft to complete the essay's diagnostic move: practical content and outcomes are identical in delegated acts, but the expressive dimension — what the act communicates about the person's moral seriousness — is not.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một loại hành động mà hiệu quả kỹ thuật không thể cứu vãn. Người ta có thể thuê một người chăm sóc có kỹ năng; người ta không thể thuê ai đó đau buồn thay mình. Không phải mọi thứ có thể thuê ngoài đều nên thuê ngoài, và lý do không phải lúc nào cũng về chất lượng kết quả — mà là về việc kết quả thuộc về ai.

Mặc định hiện đại ủng hộ uỷ quyền bất cứ nơi nào uỷ quyền có hiệu quả. Logic này đúng khi áp dụng cho các hành động thực sự có thể thay thế. Nhưng không phải tất cả hành động đều như vậy. Một số là những gì có thể gọi là không thể rút gọn ở ngôi thứ nhất: ý nghĩa của chúng nằm chính xác ở thực tế rằng chúng được thực hiện bởi người phù hợp với chúng.

Lời xin lỗi không chỉ là thông tin mà là một hình thức tự phục tùng. Người xin lỗi vẫn ở khoảng cách thoải mái và gửi đại diện của họ vào sự khó chịu mà họ nợ. Những gì đến không phải là lời xin lỗi mà là thông điệp về một lời xin lỗi.

Cứ cho là, lý do thực tế cho việc uỷ quyền thường rất thuyết phục. Dù vậy, lý do thực tế không làm cạn kiệt lý do đạo đức. Khi một người đi sáu tiếng để ở bên giường bệnh của cha mẹ đang hấp hối, hành trình là một phần của hành động — không phải vì nó tạo ra kết quả tốt hơn mà vì nó làm cho trọng lượng của mối quan hệ thành hình một cách cụ thể.

Những gì uỷ quyền loại bỏ khỏi một hành động không phải lúc nào cũng thấy rõ trong kết quả mà trong chiều kích biểu đạt. Người quản lý uỷ quyền cuộc trò chuyện sa thải cho phòng nhân sự đang đưa ra tuyên bố: bạn không được hưởng sự hiện diện của tôi trong điều này.

Chính chiều kích biểu đạt của một số hành động, không phải nội dung thực tế của chúng, mới giải thích tại sao uỷ quyền cảm thấy như sự né tránh khi không nên như vậy. Các kết quả có thể giống hệt nhau; ý nghĩa thì không.

Câu hỏi cần thiết hỏi không phải là tính toán hiệu quả mà là câu hỏi về quyền sở hữu đạo đức: hành động này của ai, và điều đó có nghĩa gì khi người đó nhờ người khác làm thay vì tự làm?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say makes some acts 'irreducibly first-personal'?
options: They require specialised first-person skill that others cannot acquire
Their significance lies in being performed by the person to whom they morally belong, not by a substitute
They can only be described from a first-person perspective
They relate to the private life of the individual and cannot involve others
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says some acts are 'irreducibly first-personal: their significance lies precisely in the fact that they are performed by the person for whom they are appropriate, and not by a substitute.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence applies fronted negation for argumentative force?
options: Everything that can be outsourced should not always be outsourced.
Not everything that can be outsourced should be, and the reason is not always efficiency but meaning.
Outsourcing should not be the default option in every case.
The reason for not outsourcing is not always efficiency.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not everything that can be outsourced should be' places the negation first, creating a strong contrarian opening rhythm characteristic of C1 argumentative prose.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places the negation at the very beginning of the sentence for maximum rhetorical effect?

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay describe what a delegated apology delivers?
options: A less sincere but still valid form of apology
A message about an apology — not an apology in the full moral sense
An apology that may be more carefully worded than one delivered in person
An apology that relieves the recipient of the discomfort of a face-to-face encounter
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says: 'What arrives is not an apology but a message about an apology.' The apology's moral character — self-submission, accepting discomfort — is absent when delegated.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the third paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the 'Granted… Even so…' structure concede and counter?
options: Grants that apologies are difficult; counters that they must still be delivered in person
Grants that the practical case for delegation is often overwhelming; counters that the practical case does not exhaust the moral case
Grants that some acts can be delegated; counters that most cannot
Grants that delegation is efficient; counters that efficiency is the wrong standard
mcQuestions.explanation: The concession grants that 'time, skill, access, resources all conspire to make delegation rational'; the counter ('Even so') insists the practical case 'does not exhaust the moral case.'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which uses a cleft to identify what explains the evasion feeling?
options: What explains why delegation feels like evasion is equally the practical content and the expressive dimension.
It is the expressive dimension of certain acts, not their practical content, that explains why delegation feels like an evasion when it should not be.
The practical content, not the expressive dimension, best explains why delegation feels like evasion.
Delegation feels like evasion because of both practical and expressive failures equally.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is the expressive dimension of certain acts, not their practical content, that explains why delegation feels like an evasion when it should not be' uses the 'It is X, not Y, that Z' cleft to isolate the explanatory factor.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is … not … that …' in the sixth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the expressive dimension' of an act mean?
options: The artistic or creative element of an action
What the act communicates about the actor's moral seriousness — beyond its practical content or outcome
The way an act is expressed or described verbally
The emotional expression that accompanies an act
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the expressive dimension is 'the part of the act that communicates what the actor takes the situation to require of them' — it is the message the act sends about the moral weight the actor assigns to the situation.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say the six-hour journey to a dying parent's bedside communicates?
options: That the person values face-to-face communication over digital alternatives
That the person's presence is more comforting than a phone call would be
The weight the relationship had — made visible concretely and physically
That the person is willing to sacrifice their own comfort for others
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the journey 'makes visible, concretely and physically, the weight the relationship had' — not because it produces a better outcome but because it enacts the relationship's significance through physical commitment.

options: A performance that arouses strong emotions in the audience
An act performed by someone else on behalf of the person who morally should have performed it
A performance that simulates a real situation
A professional performance of a task that one would otherwise do oneself
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Vicarious performance' means doing something through or by means of another person — performing an act by proxy rather than in one's own person.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the word 'vicarious' — experiencing something through another rather than directly.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about the letter of condolence written by an assistant?
options: It contains the same propositions as one written by the employer, but lacks the evidence that the employer found the writing necessary
It is actually more effective because assistants are better writers
It communicates the same meaning as one written personally, because meaning lies in the words, not the writer
It is acceptable as long as the employer reads and approves it before sending
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says: 'A letter of condolence written by an assistant and signed by an employer contains the same propositions as one written by the employer themselves; what it does not contain is the evidence that the employer found the writing necessary.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the end of the sixth paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'moral ownership' of an act mean?
options: The legal right to perform certain acts without others' permission
The moral responsibility that attaches to a specific person, making the act theirs to perform
The ownership of moral ideas and ethical arguments
The claim that morality belongs to a particular culture or tradition
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concludes with the question of 'moral ownership: whose act is this, and what does it mean for that person to have it done for them rather than by them?' — establishing that some acts belong morally to specific people.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'accepting the cost of an act' mean in the essay's ethical framework?
options: Paying the financial costs associated with performing an act
Accepting the personal difficulty, discomfort, or sacrifice required to perform the act oneself rather than delegating it
Understanding the risks involved in any moral act
Acknowledging that some acts have unintended negative consequences
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that the moral significance of some acts 'lies precisely in its cost to the person performing it — in the fact that they had to rearrange themselves, that they had to be present in their own person, that they accepted the difficulty.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What statement does the essay say a manager makes by delegating a redundancy conversation?
options: That human resources are better trained for such conversations
That the manager is too emotionally invested to be objective
That the person being let go is not owed the manager's personal presence
That the manager respects professional boundaries
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the manager delegating a redundancy conversation is 'saying: you are not owed my presence in this' — whether or not they intend to send that message.

mcQuestions.prompt: What question does the essay say determines which acts cannot be delegated?
options: Whether the outcome would be the same if delegated
Whether delegating is practically efficient in the given circumstances
Whose act it is, and what it means for that person to have it done for them rather than by them
Whether the person being delegated to has the required skill
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay ends: 'The answer requires not efficiency calculations but a question about moral ownership: whose act is this, and what does it mean for that person to have it done for them rather than by them?'

options: Faithfulness in a romantic relationship
The quality of remaining true to what a relationship requires — including being present when presence is what is owed
The accuracy with which a relationship is described to a third party
The legal obligation to perform contracted duties in a relationship
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Relational fidelity' means being faithful to what a relationship requires — including its expressive demands, not just its practical ones. Some acts are expressions of relational fidelity that cannot be delegated without violating that fidelity.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'fidelity' means — faithfulness to what something or someone requires.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's overall position on delegation?
options: Delegation is always morally suspect and should be avoided where possible
Most delegation is right and the world depends on it — but some acts cannot be delegated without changing their moral meaning
Delegation is appropriate only for practical tasks, never for relational ones
The ethics of delegation is entirely context-dependent and cannot be generalised
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's final paragraph states: 'None of this argues against delegation in general. Most of what we delegate, we are right to delegate, and the world works because of it.' The essay targets a specific category of acts, not delegation as a practice.
mcQuestions.hint: Read the opening of the final paragraph.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay argues that some acts — apologising, being present at a deathbed, making decisions that affect others — retain their moral character only when performed by the person to whom they belong. But there is a counter-argument: in some cultures and relationships, sending a trusted proxy is itself an act of respect, not evasion. Does the essay's account of 'expressive dimension' leave room for cultural variation in what an act expresses, or does it assume a particular model of moral seriousness?
